content: Dollar-cost averaging (DCA) means investing fixed amounts at regular intervals regardless of price. Buying $500 of stock monthly means purchasing more shares when prices are low, fewer when high—reducing average cost per share vs. lump-sum at peak. DCA addresses timing risk: the danger of investing everything just before a crash. However, research shows lump-sum investing outperforms DCA about 2/3 of the time because markets trend upward—waiting to deploy cash means missing gains. DCA's value is psychological, not mathematical: it helps investors who would otherwise delay investing due to fear. Best applications: windfalls (inheritance, bonus) where investor anxiety is high, and mandatory savings (401k contributions) where DCA happens automatically.
